steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation or graph the circle as a check. For diagram facts, mark equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Find the radius of the circle
steps.content:
Since point $A$ lies on the circle with center $C(h, k)$, the radius equals the distance from $C$ to $A$.
$$r = CA = \sqrt{(h+1-h)^2 + (k+\sqrt{102}-k)^2} = \sqrt{1^2 + (\sqrt{102})^2} = \sqrt{1 + 102} = \sqrt{103}$$

steps.title: Apply the Pythagorean theorem to the right triangle
steps.content:
Point $B$ also lies on the circle, so $CB = \sqrt{103}$ (the same radius). Since $\angle ACB$ is a right angle, triangle $ACB$ is a right triangle with legs $CA$ and $CB$.

By the Pythagorean theorem:
$$AB^2 = (\sqrt{103})^2 + (\sqrt{103})^2 = 206$$
$$AB = \sqrt{206}$$
That matches Choice A — $\sqrt{206}$.
